narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on academic and background factors, estimated a 51.6% chance that you would not pass—tipping just over the threshold that leads to rejection. This prediction draws from details like your undergraduate GPA of 2.90, LSAT score of 33.50, Year 1 and Year 3 grade deciles, middle family income bracket, and full-time enrollment plans, while setting aside gender and race entirely. The three biggest factors pulling the prediction toward failure were your Year 1 decile ranking of 1st—much lower than the 6th decile average among those who passed—and your Year 3 decile of 2nd, compared to their typical 6th decile. These early and later undergraduate performance indicators stood out as the strongest signals suggesting challenges ahead on the bar exam. On a more positive note, your LSAT score pushed meaningfully in the direction of passing, countering some of that risk even though it's below the 37.27 average for successful students. Your middle income level aligns with about a third of those who passed, and opting for full-time study matches the norm for 93% of them, so those played smaller roles here. Overall, your profile compares to accepted applicants by lagging most in those key decile rankings, where stronger early grades often signal the grit needed for bar success. It's tough to hear, especially when you've pushed through with a solid LSAT and full-time commitment ahead. To turn this around, focus on boosting your academic deciles—consider retaking courses or targeted tutoring to climb those rankings, as that's the model's clearest red flag for you. Retaking the LSAT could amplify that positive influence too, aiming closer to or above the typical 37 range. Many students in similar spots have succeeded by addressing these gaps before reapplying, so use this as a roadmap to strengthen your case next time. You're capable of shifting this prediction with focused effort.
